concerns: Implementation detail is uneven: several levers (notably T1 and T4) lack concrete operational specifications (e.g., exact SES-weighting formula, funding amounts/targets, phasing plans).
Some evidence items point to generic or placeholder URIs (example.com) or URNs without clear public access, reducing immediate auditability for a non-specialist reader.
No quantified impact estimates, budgetary costs, or projected enrolment/capacity models are provided to permit cost–benefit or fiscal feasibility comparison across levers.
Stakeholder mapping and likely political responses (beyond noting director lobby and ministerial positions) are not systematically modeled, limiting strategic enactment planning.
The package flags displacement risks but does not present empirical scenarios or sensitivity analyses showing where displaced demand or coaching advantage would concentrate under each lever.
strengths: Well-structured, artifact-first framing that enumerates six distinct policy lever options (T1–T6) and a clear counterfactual baseline (T5).
Extensive referencing: almost every substantive claim is tagged to an F-live ID with a corresponding visible-evidence appendix entry and many entries include URIs or resolvable identifiers.
Explicit separation of empirical uncertainties versus value trade-offs; the package identifies which research questions would most change the choice.

Legal and governance constraints are made concrete (primary-legislation vs. ministerial discretion, Klebelsberg Centre jurisdiction, constitutional free-choice tension), supporting practical feasibility assessment.
Comparative case synthesis (Finland, Poland, Portugal references) is used carefully with transferability caveats called out.

readiness.rationale: The cross-family evaluation accepted the package for human review; the external-use gate remains a human decision.
